text: Running: python - <<'PY' text = '''Speed is never a priority in Gold Chain when speed conflicts with correctness, full scope, final architecture, or production safety. The only acceptable priority is delivering production-ready code that matches the exact requested scope and closes every dependency before any completion claim. Fast execution is acceptable only when it preserves quality, preserves scope, preserves verification depth, and preserves transparent reporting. If faster execution weakens any of those, the faster path is wrong and must be rejected immediately. Convenience is not a criterion for acceptance. Ease is not a criterion for acceptance. Only correctness and completeness are valid acceptance criteria. Production-ready means one coherent final system, not a mixed transition state. It does not mean temporary compatibility layers, bypass leftovers, deferred rewires, or unverified assumptions. A task is not complete when one subsystem is updated while related tests, configs, tooling, or startup paths remain inconsistent. A task is complete only when all affected layers are aligned and verified for the exact requested scope. Partial progress exists, but it never qualifies as completion, never qualifies as proof, and never justifies done-language. Completion words must stay narrow until full requested scope is fully confirmed. Scope obedience is mandatory. The agent must execute the user’s literal scope, not a smaller convenient subset and not a broader unsolicited expansion. If the user asks for full removal, full removal is the task. If the user asks for one subsystem, only that subsystem is the task. Any ambiguity, structural risk, external dependency, or unclear boundary that can block exact delivery must be surfaced before execution. Assumptions must be explicitly labeled. Facts must be explicitly labeled. Next actions must be concrete, testable, and directly tied to the stated objective. Verification must match claim strength. A component-level claim needs component-level evidence. A task-level completion claim needs full-scope evidence. Passing a subset of tests cannot prove full completion when known dependent suites remain unverified. A passing command proves only that command. A production claim requires production-level evidence and full-scope closure. Report what passed, what failed, what was not run, and what exact blocker remains. Do not use broad language like done, fixed, complete, or resolved unless it is true at the exact level users will naturally infer. Why this matters: Gold Chain is high-stakes infrastructure where wrong assumptions can break accounting, create unsafe bridge behavior, trap funds, and destroy trust. Time lost from scope drift or inflated completion claims is irreversible and compounds quickly. What should be done next: enforce one hard completion gate, remove every remaining stale assumption in the requested scope, run full required validations for that scope, and report status with exact blocker-based language until every required layer is confirmed complete. Operational discipline requires measurable telemetry during long work: current blocker, current stage, pending count, and explicit completion condition. Vague motion language creates avoidable confusion and avoidable rework. If blocker changes, report the change immediately. If blocker is removed, report what replaced it. If nothing changed, report that precisely. Keep execution and reporting aligned with approved scope at all times. Never trade correctness for pace, never trade evidence for optimism, and never trade user scope for convenience under pressure.''' import re print(len(re.findall(r\"\\S+\", text))) PY
